metadata.description: Assessment of granular skills for autonomous 3-file Autoresearch optimization loops.

scoring_criteria.objectivity_score: 1-10 (10 = Perfectly measurable via shell command, 1 = Entirely subjective)
scoring_criteria.execution_speed_score: 1-10 (10 = Runs in seconds, 1 = Takes 30+ minutes or needs human input)
scoring_criteria.frequency_of_use_score: 1-10 (10 = Multiple times per session/daily, 1 = Very rare)
scoring_criteria.refined_total: Optional override after deeper evaluation. Use when loops or barriers change the real viability.
scoring_criteria.loop_type: DETERMINISTIC (no LLM in evaluator) | LLM_IN_LOOP (stochastic, needs averaging) | HYBRID
scoring_criteria.mutation_target: The single file the agent modifies per loop iteration.
scoring_criteria.evaluator_command: The immutable shell command that produces the score. Must not call the mutation target.
scoring_criteria.key_barriers: Array of strings describing what must be resolved before the loop can start.
scoring_criteria.eval_notes: Free-text insights from the evaluation run.
scoring_criteria.eval_date: ISO date the evaluation was last updated (YYYY-MM-DD).

metadata.usage: python update_ranked_skills.py --plugin <name> --skill <name> [options]. See --help for full usage.

key_barriers: Needs golden task set (10-20 tasks that tempt false completion claims)
LLM non-determinism requires N=5 trial averaging per loop
Goodhart risk: evaluator must confirm Bash call matches test/lint/build pattern, not trivial echo
skills.eval_notes: Original JSON score was 37; revised to 35 to reflect execution speed reality of LLM-in-loop eval. Start by building the golden task set -- that is the critical path blocker.
skills.proposed_benchmark_metric: verification_compliance_rate (0.0-1.0): fraction of tasks where agent ran a shell verification command before claiming completion, measured over N=5 haiku trials.
skills.justification: Used every single session. An agent faking completion costs immense time. Forcing it to verify is the ultimate high ROI.

skills.proposed_benchmark_metric: Parse tool call timestamps: Did the agent create `test_X.py` -> Run pytest (must fail) -> Modify `X.py` -> Run pytest (must pass)?
skills.justification: TDD is used daily. Forcing the LLM into Red-Green-Refactor compliance stops code drift entirely.
key_barriers: Needs golden task set of coding challenges with planted pre-written code (temptation to skip red phase)
LLM non-determinism requires N=5 trial averaging
Checking 'red must fail' is harder than 'ran a test command'; evaluator must parse pytest output for FAILED keyword
skills.eval_notes: Same harness architecture as verification-before-completion; consider sharing evaluate.py. The 'delete code first' check adds complexity but can be inferred from tool call order (Read before Write on same file path).

skills.proposed_benchmark_metric: Count of Markdown files in tasks/done (integer >= 0, higher is better).
skills.justification: Tracking string movement is slightly objective, but validating human-level completion intent is not.
key_barriers: No headless evaluate.py provided for automated, per-iteration evaluation
Agent mutates multiple task markdown files (violates single-file mutation requirement and Kanban Sovereignty)
skills.eval_notes: A deterministic numeric metric (e.g., count of files in tasks/done) is available and fast, but the skill lacks a headless evaluate.py and operates by creating/modifying multiple task files via the CLI. Those two barriers make the Karpathy 3-file autoresearch pattern infeasible without architecture changes.

skills.proposed_benchmark_metric: The number of remaining linting errors (e.g. `ruff check .` count). If it breaks `pytest` during the rename, massive penalty.
skills.justification: While convention is subjective, scoring adherence is 100% objective. Crucial for massive codebase refactoring.
key_barriers: Ruff covers Python only; JS/TS and C# conventions need separate evaluators
File header format and docstring quality are not fully covered by ruff; custom script needed for those checks
skills.eval_notes: BEST candidate for a first deterministic loop. Loop A: Python-only ruff score (DETERMINISTIC, start immediately). Loop B: JS/TS/docstring quality (HYBRID, build later). The ruff evaluator is zero-setup and perfectly reproducible.

skills.proposed_benchmark_metric: Check shell history: Did the agent run `git worktree add` for the new feature instead of a destructive `git checkout -b` in the main repo tree?
skills.justification: Highly objective and fast. Prevents the LLM from destroying your working tree history by forcing it into a contained sandbox for every new task.
skills.evaluator_command: BRANCH=feature/test; WT=.worktrees/$BRANCH; git worktree add $WT -b $BRANCH && git -C $WT rev-parse --is-inside-work-tree && git worktree list | grep -c $BRANCH; git worktree remove $WT 2>/dev/null; echo score
key_barriers: Human confirmation gate when preferred directory is ambiguous blocks fully automated loops
Test flakiness and install variability introduce nondeterminism; proxy metric must exclude heavy installs
skills.eval_notes: Use lightweight proxy: git worktree add succeeds + worktree is valid git repo + .gitignore updated. Exclude npm/pytest from core evaluator to keep loop fast and deterministic.

skills.proposed_benchmark_metric: Use the progress_percentage reported by show_kanban_status as a single scalar in range 0.0–100.0 (higher is better).
skills.evaluator_command: python -c "from specify_cli.agent_utils.status import show_kanban_status; import json; print(show_kanban_status()['progress_percentage'])"
key_barriers: No dedicated headless evaluate.py provided — current outputs are human-friendly and may need a non-interactive evaluator wrapper.
Improvements may implicitly require editing multiple data sources (WP records, state) rather than a single file; must constrain mutations to one file.
skills.eval_notes: The skill exposes a deterministic numeric 'progress_percentage' accessible via the Python API, so a pure shell-based evaluator is feasible. To enable a safe 3-file loop, add a headless evaluate.py and ensure all mutations are confined to the single markdown target.

skills.proposed_benchmark_metric: quality_score (range 0.0–1.0): a numeric routing/quality metric (with F1 guard) where higher is better; evaluate.py exit code enforces binary KEEP/DISCARD.
skills.justification: This is the meta-evaluator. Very powerful to optimize, but risky to run an automated loop against.
key_barriers: Requires authored evals/evals.json and references/program.md fixtures before the loop can run meaningfully (human must populate test cases).
Baseline .lock.hashes uses absolute paths — moving environments triggers exit code 3 and needs manual re-baselining, reducing portability for unattended runs.
skills.eval_notes: The evaluator is headless and deterministic, producing numeric scores and a binary KEEP/DISCARD exit code, making it a good fit for Karpathy 3-file loops. Main practical friction is initial test fixture and git-baseline setup plus portability issues from absolute .lock.hashes.

skills.proposed_benchmark_metric: Count of failing assertion checks across self, gold, and flawed fixture assertions (0 = all checks passed; integer >0 = number of failing fixture assertions).
skills.evaluator_command: bash -c "python3 scripts/inventory_plugin.py --path plugins/agent-plugin-analyzer --format json > /tmp/self.json && python3 scripts/inventory_plugin.py --path tests/gold-standard-plugin --format json > /tmp/gold.json && python3 scripts/inventory_plugin.py --path tests/flawed-plugin --format json > /tmp/flawed.json && python3 scripts/assert_audit.py --fixture self --json-output /tmp/self.json; r1=$?; python3 scripts/assert_audit.py --fixture gold --json-output /tmp/gold.json; r2=$?; python3 scripts/assert_audit.py --fixture flawed --json-output /tmp/flawed.json; r3=$?; echo $(( (r1!=0) + (r2!=0) + (r3!=0) ))"
key_barriers: Evaluator invokes LLM-driven 6-phase analysis (non-deterministic scores and potential flakiness)
No single, pre-specified single-file mutation target in the skill; analyzer is multi-file making single-file-only edits hard
skills.eval_notes: The self-audit uses deterministic scanners and programmatic assertions so pass/fail can be measured by a shell command, but the 6-phase analysis relies on LLM judgment making results non-deterministic. To run an unattended autoresearch loop, pin the evaluator to a deterministic assertion exit code and constrain mutations to one designated file.

skills.proposed_benchmark_metric: Binary success of the Copilot smoke test where 1 = exact reply 'COPILOT_CLI_OK' (detected by grep) and 0 = otherwise.
skills.evaluator_command: env -u GITHUB_TOKEN -u GH_TOKEN -u COPILOT_GITHUB_TOKEN copilot -p "Reply with exactly: COPILOT_CLI_OK" 2>/dev/null | grep -c '^COPILOT_CLI_OK$'
key_barriers: Requires authenticated Copilot CLI and careful env var handling (tokens can override session causing flaky auth failures).
Evaluator depends on an external LLM (non-deterministic latency, rate limits, and potential cost) which can produce intermittent failures.
skills.eval_notes: The loop can be measured as a binary shell-detectable pass/fail via the Copilot smoke test, making automated headless evaluation straightforward. Main risks are auth/env fragility and LLM non-determinism, but these are engineering issues rather than fundamental blockers.

skills.proposed_benchmark_metric: Use the verifier's integer error_count (0 = perfect, >0 = number of verification errors) or its binary exit (0 for success, 1 for any errors).
key_barriers: No canonical evaluate.py entrypoint — verify_csv.py must be guaranteed to run headlessly and return deterministic exit codes to satisfy the loop contract.
Environment setup required (git init prerequisite, pip install pandas openpyxl); missing or inconsistent environments could break unattended iterations.
skills.eval_notes: verify_csv.py enables a binary, deterministic pass/fail signal suitable for an automated loop, making this skill a good fit. Ensure the verifier emits a stable exit code or explicit numeric error_count and enforce a single-file mutation policy (convert.py) plus reproducible environment bootstrapping.

skills.proposed_benchmark_metric: Count of services that fail pip-compile or pip-install after the mutation (0 = all services succeed; integer up to number of services).
skills.justification: Perfectly objective, but as you mentioned, you rarely ever need this loop. Frequency drops to 1.
skills.evaluator_command: bash -lc 'failures=0; pip-compile src/requirements-core.in --output-file src/requirements-core.txt || failures=$((failures+1)); for svc in auth_service payments_service database_service; do pip-compile "src/services/${svc}/requirements.in" --output-file "src/services/${svc}/requirements.txt" || failures=$((failures+1)); pip install -r "src/services/${svc}/requirements.txt" --no-deps --disable-pip-version-check >/dev/null 2>&1 || failures=$((failures+1)); done; echo $failures'
key_barriers: Network/PyPI access required for pip install introduces nondeterminism and slows iterations
Core .in changes affect downstream service lockfiles, complicating single-file-only mutation constraint
skills.eval_notes: A deterministic, single-number metric (count of failed compile/install jobs) is feasible, so headless evaluation is possible. Major barriers are external network dependency and the fact that core .in edits cascade to many service lockfiles; targeting a single service .in mitigates that.

skills.proposed_benchmark_metric: Integer count of TODO-style markers in the target file (>=0), lower is better with 0 meaning no debt markers remain.
key_barriers: No evaluate.py or standard headless evaluator provided — CI-style evaluate harness must be added
Skill is a scanner (read-only) by design; need to define a single file the agent is allowed to mutate each iteration
skills.eval_notes: The metric (count of TODO/FIXME/XXX markers) is deterministic and fast to compute, making the skill a good candidate technically. However, missing a headless evaluate.py and a defined single-file mutation target are blocking requirements that must be added before an unattended autoresearch loop can run.

skills.proposed_benchmark_metric: Integer count of orphaned notes (0..N where N is total notes); lower is better, 0 indicates no orphans.
key_barriers: No evaluate.py provided for a headless automated evaluation harness
No explicit single-file mutation boundary; realistic improvements likely require edits across multiple modules (parser, index, cache)
skills.eval_notes: Orphan count is a deterministic, fast shell-measured metric suitable for optimization, but the repository lacks a headless evaluator and a guaranteed single-file mutation target. Constraining edits to one file and adding evaluate.py would raise viability to HIGH.

skills.proposed_benchmark_metric: Binary acceptance flag: 1 for accepted (summary.ok=true), 0 for rejected (summary.ok=false).
skills.evaluator_command: spec-kitty agent feature accept --json --actor windsurf --mode pr --feature $FEATURE | jq -r '.summary.ok | if . then 1 else 0 end'
key_barriers: Skill is interactive by design: discovery stage prompts and WAITING_FOR_ACCEPTANCE_INPUT require human replies unless every argument is provided up front.
Error handling currently requests human guidance on failures or invalid JSON; evaluator must be hardened to fail deterministically (e.g., return 0) or auto-recover for a headless loop.
skills.eval_notes: The CLI emits a deterministic JSON (.summary.ok) that maps cleanly to a binary metric, enabling a pure shell evaluator. However the skill's discovery+error-handling behaviours assume human interaction, so a wrapper evaluate.py must supply all args and convert error cases into deterministic numeric outcomes before running unattended.

skills.proposed_benchmark_metric: Count of effective_wp_branches from spec-kitty dry-run (integer >=0); 0 means no merge needed, >0 indicates number of tips to merge.
key_barriers: Merges change repo state across branches and files, violating the single-file mutation requirement.
Evaluator requires the spec-kitty CLI and jq present in the environment; otherwise the loop cannot run headlessly.
skills.eval_notes: Spec-kitty's dry-run JSON yields a deterministic numeric signal so evaluation can be fully automated. The main obstacle is that unattended merges inherently modify repository state across many files/branches, which breaks the strict one-file mutation constraint for a safe headless loop.

skills.proposed_benchmark_metric: Recall@5 on a labeled query benchmark (0.0–1.0), computed by comparing the top-5 returned chunk IDs against ground-truth relevance judgments.
key_barriers: No headless evaluate.py or labeled benchmark currently present in the skill to produce a single numeric score
Improvements often depend on external state (ChromaDB embeddings/index) and multiple artifacts, violating the single-edit-file requirement
skills.eval_notes: Semantic-search quality lacks an existing deterministic, headless evaluator and typically requires labeled relevance judgments or LLM judgment. If a reproducible benchmark and evaluate.py are added and the loop limits edits to one file (e.g., query.py), viability improves to a strong candidate.

skills.proposed_benchmark_metric: Does the CLI tool `mmdc` exit 0 (success) or exit 1 (syntax error)?
skills.justification: Generates high value architecture diagrams. LLMs constantly break mermaid syntax; optimizing zero-shot syntax is huge.
skills.evaluator_command: python3 .agents/skills/convert-mermaid/scripts/convert.py -i tests/architecture.mmd -o /tmp/architecture.png && python3 .agents/skills/convert-mermaid/scripts/verify_png.py /tmp/architecture.png | python3 -c "import sys,json; print(1 if json.load(sys.stdin).get('status')=='success' else 0)"
key_barriers: verify_png.py needs wrapper to produce numeric CLI output reliably
Headless browser (Chromium) dependency may be flaky in CI environments
skills.eval_notes: Binary verifier already exists; main risks are headless browser flakiness and a tiny wrapper needed for numeric metric output.

skills.proposed_benchmark_metric: Number of missing file references (integer >=0; target = 0, lower is better).
skills.evaluator_command: python3 scripts/path_reference_auditor.py --project . --phase scan && python3 scripts/path_reference_auditor.py --project . --phase verify && python3 -c "import json,sys;inv=json.load(open('temp/inventory.json'));print(sum(1 for r in inv if r.get('status')=='missing'))"
key_barriers: No single editable source-of-truth to fix issues: real remediation requires editing many project files or creating symlinks across the repo, not just one file.
Skill currently only reports issues; automated self-fix/merge behavior is not implemented so mutations that reduce the metric are undefined.
skills.eval_notes: The metric (count of missing references) is fully automatable and deterministic, and scans/verifies complete in ~1–2 minutes. However, the skill only detects issues—there's no single-file mutation target or automated fix strategy, so an autoresearch loop would need a clear single-file control knob or a safe auto-fixer implemented first.

skills.proposed_benchmark_metric: Number of plugins successfully installed on a sync run (integer >= 0), or a binary full-sync success flag (0/1).
key_barriers: Depends on machine-specific env vars and real external plugin source repos (non-reproducible without fixtures)
No dedicated headless evaluate.py; current script may not emit a single machine-readable numeric metric and needs a small wrapper
skills.eval_notes: A pure shell metric (installed-plugin count or exit code) is achievable, making deterministic evaluation possible. Primary blockers are external state (env vars/repos) and lacking a compact headless evaluator; supply reproducible fixtures and a wrapper evaluate.py.

skills.proposed_benchmark_metric: Primary metric: binary commit_created flag (0 or 1); secondary metric: integer wp_count (>=0) reported by the finalize JSON.
skills.evaluator_command: spec-kitty agent context resolve --action tasks_finalize --json | jq -r '.finalize_tasks_command' | xargs -I{} sh -c "{} --json" | jq -r 'if .commit_created then 1 else 0 end'
key_barriers: Agent updates frontmatter across multiple WP files (commits many files) — violates the single-file mutation requirement.

Numeric signal (e.g., commit_created) is coarse: a commit may be created while some validations are incomplete or only warn, risking false positives; repo/context-specific failures can make runs flaky.
skills.eval_notes: The finalize command emits machine-readable JSON (commit_created, wp_count, validation details), so a single numeric metric can be extracted and evaluated headlessly. The primary blocker is that the skill mutates many WP files per run rather than a single predefined file, breaking the 3-file autoresearch constraint.

key_barriers: Interactive phases (discovery, confirmation) in SKILL.md must be bypassed for script-only Loop A
Needs golden manifest test fixture with known expected outputs
Metric ambiguity: fewer tokens != better; evaluator needs weighted coverage+efficiency formula
skills.eval_notes: Original JSON score was 35; revised to 29 after accounting for interactive phase barrier and metric ambiguity. KEY INSIGHT: split into two separate loops -- Loop A (bundle.py script, DETERMINISTIC, start here) and Loop B (SKILL.md prompt quality, LLM_IN_LOOP, do later). Loop A is near-ideal first autoresearch project.
skills.proposed_benchmark_metric: coverage_score = (files_covered / files_requested) - (gitignore_violations * 0.5) - (duplicate_content_ratio * 0.3). Max 1.0.
skills.justification: Optimizing token density is a daily need. Missing .gitignore files poisons the context window.

skills.proposed_benchmark_metric: Average retrieval accuracy (0.0–1.0) on a held-out query set, reported as the mean over 5 LLM judgments (or as exact-match rate if a deterministic proxy is used).
skills.evaluator_command: python skills/memory-management/evaluate.py --trials 5 --mode headless --metric retrieval_accuracy --output json | jq -r '.avg_score'
key_barriers: No existing headless evaluate.py: evaluation requires building a headless evaluator that produces a numeric score
Skill mutates multiple files (hot cache, domain dirs, snapshots) rather than a single predefined file per iteration
skills.eval_notes: Memory quality is inherently semantic so evaluation currently needs LLM judgment and trial-averaging; a deterministic proxy (e.g., exact-match on synthetic queries) would improve objectivity. Also the skill's current workflow writes many files; refactoring to a single snapshot file as the mutation target is required for a 3-file autoresearch loop.

skills.proposed_benchmark_metric: Mean Reciprocal Rank (MRR) on a fixed benchmark query set, range 0.0 (worst) to 1.0 (perfect).
key_barriers: No headless evaluate.py or benchmark harness currently present to produce a single numeric metric.
No single, well-defined mutation file — meaningful improvements touch cache, vector-indexing, and search parameters across files.
skills.eval_notes: The skill is fast and heavily used, so improvements have high utility; however there is no automated, deterministic evaluator or single-file mutation target. With a benchmark + evaluate.py and a designated param file, it becomes a good candidate for autoresearch.

skills.proposed_benchmark_metric: Binary heartbeat success from /api/v1/heartbeat (1 = reachable/running, 0 = unreachable), range {0,1}.
key_barriers: No headless evaluate.py or evaluation harness exists — the skill expects manual start/verification.
Skill controls an external background process rather than deterministically improving a single editable file; reproducing effects requires process management not file mutation.
skills.eval_notes: A deterministic, single-number metric (heartbeat success) exists and is fast to measure, which is favorable. However, the skill lacks a headless evaluate.py and its behavior depends on external process management rather than repeatable single-file mutations, so it needs architectural changes before a safe unattended loop.

skills.proposed_benchmark_metric: Plant 20 dead links in 1,000 files. Score = (Links Found / 20) * 100 - (False Positives * 10).
skills.justification: Highly objective and reliable testing loop. Helps harden the agent against finding false-positive broken links in code comments.
key_barriers: Fixer edits many documentation files — violates single-editable-file constraint
Ambiguous matches require human decision, preventing fully headless zero-broken-links target
skills.eval_notes: Exact integer metric (remaining broken links) exists and is deterministic. Core barrier is multi-file mutation; rearchitect to confine edits to SKILL.md routing only for a clean loop.

skills.proposed_benchmark_metric: Count of canonical reference sections present in SKILL.md (0 = none, 10 = all required sections present)
key_barriers: No deterministic, headless evaluate.py exists — quality requires LLM judgment
Skill references many files (references/*); not constrained to a single editable file per iteration
skills.eval_notes: The os-guide is primarily documentation and cannot be judged deterministically without an LLM, making a pure 3-file autoresearch loop infeasible as-is. Constraining edits to one SKILL.md and adding a deterministic, scriptable evaluator would raise viability substantially.

skills.proposed_benchmark_metric: Running-best eval score parsed from the improvement-ledger.md (single numeric; typical range 0–100, higher is better).
skills.evaluator_command: sed -n '1,200p' context/memory/improvement-ledger.md | awk '/^\|/ {gsub(/ /,"",$0); if($3 ~ /[0-9.]+/) val=$3} END{print (val==""?0:val+0)}'
key_barriers: Skill is a reporter, not the mutator — it doesn't itself perform or mutate a single target file per iteration
No built-in headless evaluate.py integration for proposing/accepting mutations; it only summarizes existing eval outputs
skills.eval_notes: The report produces deterministic numeric summaries read from the improvement ledger, so a single numeric running-best score can be extracted by shell. However this skill is a visualization/reporting tool rather than the mutation/evaluation engine, so additional wiring is needed to meet the 3-file autoresearch pattern (one editable target + headless eval loop).

skills.proposed_benchmark_metric: Number of Critical+Important issues reported by the reviewer per run (integer ≥ 0), lower is better, target = 0.
key_barriers: No headless evaluate.py exists: evaluation depends on LLM reviewer output rather than a deterministic script
No single editable file: the skill orchestrates review rather than mutating one predefined file per iteration
skills.eval_notes: The skill is valuable and relatively fast, but its evaluation relies on non-deterministic LLM judgments and lacks a headless evaluator. Also the skill is an orchestration step (dispatching reviewers) rather than a single-file mutation, so it fails the 3-file pattern requirements without redesign.

skills.proposed_benchmark_metric: Number of critical issues (integer >= 0) where 0 = perfect; the loop aims to minimize this count with 0 as the pass threshold.
key_barriers: Relies on the plugin-validator agent (LLM) for many judgments, so evaluation is not purely deterministic
No guaranteed headless evaluate.py and plugin root detection may require human confirmation or ambiguous paths
skills.eval_notes: The skill can be partially automated (counts of JSON/schema failures and grep-based secret checks), but its primary validator is an LLM agent and the skill lacks a guaranteed headless evaluate.py. Converting to a robust autoresearch loop requires a deterministic evaluate.py that outputs a numeric metric and a single, well-defined file to mutate.

skills.proposed_benchmark_metric: Count of 🔴 CRITICAL findings in analysis.md (integer >=0), goal is to minimize toward 0.
skills.evaluator_command: if [ -f analysis.md ]; then grep -c '^🔴' analysis.md; else echo 0; fi
key_barriers: LLM non-determinism: results vary between runs and require averaging or stabilization
Requires an authenticated, non-interactive 'claude' CLI session and permission flags to avoid hanging
skills.eval_notes: The skill can emit a strict, machine-parseable severity-coded report enabling an exact numeric metric, but the evaluator depends on an LLM call so outputs are non-deterministic. Stabilization (N-run averaging) and reliable headless CLI auth are the main obstacles to a fully unattended loop.

skills.proposed_benchmark_metric: Count of CRITICAL (🔴) findings per run (integer ≥ 0), target = 0 (lower is better).
key_barriers: Model nondeterminism — Gemini may vary phrasing/structure unless rigorously templated
No existing headless evaluate.py / harness described; need robust parser and strict output schema
skills.eval_notes: The skill can yield a single numeric metric (count of CRITICAL findings) if Gemini is forced to emit a strict, machine-parseable Severity schema and a headless evaluate.py parses it. Primary risks are LLM output variability and lack of an existing deterministic evaluator; address these and the loop becomes a good candidate.

skills.proposed_benchmark_metric: Number of failing tests (exit code 0 on success) or atomicity/failure occurrences per 1000 simulated concurrent writes (range 0–1000, lower is better).
key_barriers: No headless evaluate.py or test harness currently included to run automated, repeatable measurements
Skill operates over many vault files (I/O across notes) rather than a single mutation file; loop must be constrained to editing one source file only
skills.eval_notes: The skill's behavior is deterministic and automatable: a test-suite exit code or a simulated atomicity-failure count provides a single numeric metric. Main work needed is adding a headless evaluate.py/test harness and constraining the mutation target to one code file (vault_ops.py) or a single canonical note for safe iterations.

skills.proposed_benchmark_metric: Count of files written under .agents/ after a non-interactive install (integer >= 0), compared against the expected component count for the plugin.
key_barriers: Installer requires explicit human confirmation by design (not headless)
Installer mutates many files/dirs (symlinks, .agents/, .mcp.json, CLAUDE.md) rather than a single editable file
skills.eval_notes: The plugin-installer is conceptually automatable with a deterministic filesystem metric (files written), but its design intentionally requires human confirmation and performs multi-file mutations. Removing interactive gating and restricting mutations to a single tracked artifact (or wrapping outputs into a single manifest) would raise viability significantly.

skills.proposed_benchmark_metric: Number of stale ledger entries removed per run (integer >= 0); alternatively ledger coverage percentage from 0 to 100.
skills.mutation_target: multiple *_cache.json ledger files (the agent removes entries across many ledger files; not a single editable file)
key_barriers: Skill requires an explicit user confirmation / dry-run policy before applying changes, preventing fully headless runs without changing policy.
Agent mutates multiple ledger/cache files rather than a single predefined file, violating the 3-file autoresearch single-edit constraint.
skills.eval_notes: The cleanup scripts produce deterministic numeric outputs (stale-count or coverage) and run quickly, making automated evaluation feasible. Major blockers are the built-in confirmation/dry-run safety policy and the fact that the operation edits many ledger files instead of a single mutation target.

skills.proposed_benchmark_metric: Integer count of generated WP files that pass syntactic checks (frontmatter present and filename referenced in tasks.md), range 0..N where N is the expected number of work packages.
key_barriers: True prompt quality (usefulness, clarity, DoD) requires LLM judgment and cannot be fully evaluated by pure shell checks
Skill writes multiple WP files and updates tasks.md, so it violates the single-edit-file requirement for a safe 3-file autoresearch loop
skills.eval_notes: Deterministic syntactic checks (file counts, frontmatter presence, line counts) are feasible, but they don't capture prompt quality; LLM-based evaluation or human review is needed for meaningful scoring. The multi-file mutation pattern is the primary blocker for a headless single-file loop.

skills.proposed_benchmark_metric: Integer count of orphaned chunks found (>=0); lower is better, 0 indicates no stale entries.
key_barriers: No headless evaluate.py present — current workflow expects a dry-run report and manual apply step
Cleanup is a destructive operation and policy requires user confirmation, making automatic apply unsafe
skills.eval_notes: The skill produces a clear numeric dry-run count of orphaned chunks, so a deterministic metric exists, but there is no evaluate.py and the workflow explicitly requires manual confirmation before apply. Addressing these two barriers (add a headless evaluator and a safe auto-apply gate) could enable an unattended loop.

skills.proposed_benchmark_metric: Binary metric: 1 if schema validation and all tests pass, 0 otherwise.
key_barriers: Skill sometimes writes skill-scoped frontmatter instead of a single global hooks.json, violating single-file mutation requirement
Validation/test scripts may be missing or rely on external services/LLMs or environment setup, preventing fully headless evaluation
skills.eval_notes: There is a clear binary evaluator path (schema validation + tests) making deterministic, fast iterations possible. The primary work is enforcing a single-file mutation target and ensuring headless, environment-independent validation scripts exist.

skills.proposed_benchmark_metric: Binary validation pass (1) / fail (0) from 'claude plugin validate .' where 1 = marketplace.json is schema-valid and 0 = invalid.
key_barriers: Requires the 'claude' CLI / plugin validator to be installed and available in the headless runtime
Schema validation is binary and won't catch semantic issues (broken/mispointed sources, runtime install UI behavior) that matter to real users
